- Check if the arrays are null or different lengths, but according to the problem statement, they are of equal length, so no need.

- Create two int arrays of size 1001, initialized to zero.

- For each element in target, increment freqTarget[element].

- For each element in arr, increment freqArr[element].

- Compare the two frequency arrays. If all elements are the same, return true; else, false.

Wait, but in Java, comparing two arrays with '==' checks if they are the same object, not if their contents are the same. So, we need to loop through each index and compare the counts.

So, in code:

public boolean canBeEqual(int[] target, int[] arr) {
    int[] freqTarget = new int[1001];
    int[] freqArr = new int[1001];
    
    for (int num : target) {
        freqTarget[num]++;
    }
    for (int num : arr) {
        freqArr[num]++;
    }
    
    for (int i = 0; i < 1001; i++) {
        if (freqTarget[i] != freqArr[i]) {
            return false;
        }
    }
    return true;
}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public boolean canBeEqual(int[] target, int[] arr) {
        int[] freqTarget = new int[1001];
        int[] freqArr = new int[1001];
        
        for (int num : target) {
            freqTarget[num]++;
        }
        for (int num : arr) {
            freqArr[num]++;
        }
        
        for (int i = 0; i < 1001; i++) {
            if (freqTarget[i] != freqArr[i]) {
                return false;
            }
        }
        return true;
    }
}
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution checks if both arrays have the same elements with the same frequencies by using frequency arrays. This approach ensures that we can determine if one array can be transformed into the other through any number of reverses, as the order of elements can be rearranged freely. The time complexity is linear, making it efficient for the given constraints.
